flags.note: Carrier 'The dog can run very ___.' has no disambiguating context, so all three distractors yield fully grammatical, true sentences: 'can run very well', 'can run very slowly', 'can run very badly'. Only the intended-target convention (fast = irregular adverb) separates 'fast' from 'well'; 'The dog can run very well' is unimpeachable English. Grader does exact button match, so a defensible pick is mis-graded.
flags.suggestion: Add context that forces fast (e.g. 'It won the race. It can run very ___.') or swap 'well' for a clearly-wrong option.

flags.note: Carrier 'Please close the door ___. The child is in bed.' -> quietly. Distractors 'slowly' and 'nicely' are grammatical, sensible imperatives ('close the door slowly' to avoid noise is defensible given the same context). Context cues quietly but does not exclude them.
flags.suggestion: Replace 'slowly'/'nicely' with manner adverbs that clash with the context (e.g. 'loudly', 'angrily').

flags.note: Carrier 'She did her homework ___ because she wanted to watch TV.' -> quickly. Distractor 'badly' is grammatical and plausibly motivated by the same clause (rushing to watch TV -> did it badly). 'She did her homework badly because she wanted to watch TV' reads as correct English.
flags.suggestion: Swap 'badly' for an option the 'wanted to watch TV' motive cannot license (e.g. 'beautifully', 'carefully').

flags.note: Carrier 'It's cold! I ___ close the window.' -> will (spontaneous decision). Distractor 'can' yields the natural, grammatical offer 'I can close the window.' Same systemic 'can' modal-distractor issue recurs in will gf.002 and (as 'can't') gf.006; each produces a grammatical alternative reading. Grader exact-matches the button, so 'can' is mis-graded as wrong despite being acceptable English.
flags.suggestion: Replace 'can' with a distractor that is ungrammatical in the slot (e.g. 'be', 'to') across gf.001/gf.002, and reconsider 'can't' in gf.006.

flags.note: Carrier 'Don't worry, I ___ help you with your homework.' -> will. Distractor 'can' yields the natural, grammatical offer 'I can help you with your homework.' Defensible alternative reading; same pattern as gf.001.
flags.suggestion: Replace 'can' with an option that is ungrammatical before the bare infinitive (e.g. 'be', 'to').

flags.note: Carrier 'It's a big job. We ___ be at school before nine.' -> won't. Distractor 'can't' yields 'We can't be at school before nine,' which is grammatical and arguably MORE natural for inability given 'It's a big job.' Strong ambiguity between negated-future and negated-ability.
flags.suggestion: Replace 'can't' with an option that is ungrammatical here (e.g. 'aren't going' is too advanced; use 'don't'/'aren't' only and drop 'can't').
